text.title: onboarding-60-90-day Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for onboarding-60-90-day. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: The Contribute phase ends when the hire has owned and completed at least one project end-to-end; "participating" or "contributing to" a team project does not satisfy this criterion.
rule.rationale: End-to-end ownership reveals planning, execution, communication, and handoff capability. Participation in a team project reveals only the ability to execute a defined slice; it does not demonstrate the full delivery loop that the Execute phase depends on.

rule.statement: Confirm all five day-60 success criteria are met before allowing the hire to enter the Execute phase; missing criteria require a plan for resolution, not a deferral to day-90.
rule.rationale: Deferring unmet 60-day criteria to the 90-day review compresses the Execute phase into the remaining time and produces a rushed, low-evidence review. Addressing gaps at day 60 while there is still 30 days of execution time is the correct intervention point.

rule.statement: The Execute phase measures independence: the hire must handle most responsibilities without needing guidance; if guidance is still required for familiar tasks at day 90, the hire has not met the ramp bar.
rule.rationale: The 90-day review is the formal transition from onboarding to standard performance management. A hire who still requires frequent guidance at day 90 is on an extended ramp that must be named explicitly and managed differently than the standard onboarding conclusion.

rule.statement: Include a mentorship milestone only if there is a confirmed newer hire to mentor; never add it as a generic aspiration.
rule.rationale: Generic mentorship milestones at day 90 fail when there are no newer hires; the milestone becomes unfulfillable and the review is forced to waive it, undermining the credibility of the other criteria.

rule.statement: Run a skip-level check-in at day 45; the manager is not the only signal source and may have blind spots the skip-level surfaces.
rule.rationale: Manager-only feedback produces biased signals: managers unconsciously minimize problems they contributed to and amplify successes they credit themselves for. The skip-level provides an independent calibration point mid-ramp.
